Diff Drift · engine benchmark

One exam, every release

The engine eval is a deterministic CI gate: each case is a real temporary git repo with one planted problem the binary must flag and clean decoys it must stay quiet on. A failing case blocks the merge, so the published binary always passes the full suite.

Engine eval
117/117
cases · CI blocker · exact flags, counts, exit codes

Coverage — cases by family

Counted from eval/cases/ at build time. Each family covers the rules whose concept exists there, plus a structural-drift noise case.

JS/TS
20
Python
16
Java
14
Kotlin
14
Rust
13
C#
12
Swift
11
Go
10
Dependency manifests
7
passing required — red is unreleasable

Quiet — flags on benign real-repo ranges

8 OSS repos, last 25 routine upstream commits each (770 changed files). Every flag grades Diff Drift's noise, not these projects — the ranges were already reviewed and merged upstream.

clap (Rust) 56 files
1
gorilla/mux (Go) 17 files
0 — silent
requests (Python) 22 files
0 — silent
gson (Java) 51 files
14
okio (Kotlin) 43 files
0 — silent
Newtonsoft.Json (C#) 99 files
1
Alamofire (Swift) 403 files
5
vercel/turbo (monorepo: pnpm + cargo) 79 files
8
security-rule flags (9) dependency-change notices (20, Low)
The dependency notices sit on windows that really did bump versions — accurate observations a reviewer skims in seconds, kept visible here so the quiet claim isn't propped up by hiding them. Measure your own number: npm run eval:fp-replay against branches you consider benign predicts your actual triage cost.
Engine suite counted from the working tree · noise measured 2026-06-12 by scripts/fp-replay.mjs local · deterministic · no model calls
